annotations.label: Markov chain / Markov process

anchor.snippet: a Markov chain or Markov process is a stochastic process describing a sequence of possible events

annotations.note: Mathlib has Markov kernels and Ionescu-Tulcea trajectory measures (`ProbabilityTheory.trajMeasure`) that yield Markov processes by iteration, but no single bundled `MarkovProcess`/`MarkovChain` definition.

annotations.label: Discrete-time Markov chain (DTMC)

anchor.snippet: in which the chain moves state at discrete time steps, gives a discrete-time Markov chain

annotations.note: Discrete-time iteration of a Markov kernel is constructed via `traj`/`trajMeasure` in Ionescu-Tulcea, but there is no top-level `DTMC` structure.

annotations.label: Continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC)

anchor.snippet: A continuous-time process is called a continuous-time Markov chain

annotations.note: No CTMC, infinitesimal generator, or continuous-time Markov semigroup is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Markov process (memorylessness)

anchor.snippet: A Markov process is a stochastic process that satisfies the Markov property

annotations.note: No `MarkovProperty` predicate on a stochastic process exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Markov chain (state/index space)

anchor.snippet: A Markov chain is a type of Markov process that has either a discrete state space or a discrete index set

annotations.note: No explicit `MarkovChain` definition distinguishing discrete state/index from general Markov processes appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Transitions and transition probabilities

anchor.snippet: The changes of state of the system are called transitions

annotations.note: Transition probabilities are exactly Markov kernels, formalized as `IsMarkovKernel κ` (every `κ x` is a probability measure).

annotations.label: Markov property (discrete-time)

anchor.snippet: The Markov property states that the conditional probability distribution for the system at the next step

annotations.note: The discrete-time Markov property is implicit in the Ionescu-Tulcea construction (`condDistrib_trajMeasure` recovers the next-step kernel), but is not stated as a standalone `MarkovProperty` predicate.

annotations.label: Coin purse non-Markov example

anchor.snippet: Suppose that there is a coin purse containing five coins worth 25

annotations.note: This pedagogical example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Markovian reformulation by coin counts

anchor.snippet: However, it is possible to model this scenario as a Markov process

annotations.note: This worked example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Discrete-time Markov chain

anchor.snippet: A discrete-time Markov chain is a sequence of random variables

annotations.note: The law of the DTMC sequence is obtainable via `trajMeasure`, but no dedicated `DTMC` structure is defined.

annotations.label: State space of the chain

anchor.snippet: The possible values of X i form a countable set S called the state space of the chain

annotations.note: There is no Markov-chain-specific notion of `stateSpace`; Mathlib kernels take an arbitrary `MeasurableSpace`.

annotations.label: Time-homogeneous Markov chain

anchor.snippet: Time-homogeneous Markov chains are processes where

annotations.note: A time-homogeneous chain corresponds to iterating one kernel `κ` via `traj`, but Mathlib has no explicit `IsTimeHomogeneous` predicate.

anchors.snippet: Time-homogeneous Markov chains are processes where

annotations.label: Stationary Markov chain

anchor.snippet: Stationary Markov chains are processes where

annotations.note: Mathlib defines invariant measures via `Kernel.Invariant κ μ` (`μ.bind κ = μ`), which is the kernel-level analogue of a stationary distribution but not a notion of a stationary stochastic process.

anchors.snippet: Stationary Markov chains are processes where

annotations.label: Stationarity implies time-homogeneity

anchor.snippet: Every stationary chain can be proved to be time-homogeneous by Bayes' rule

annotations.note: No stationary-implies-time-homogeneous theorem is in Mathlib since neither notion is defined at the process level.

anchors.snippet: Every stationary chain can be proved to be time-homogeneous by Bayes' rule

annotations.label: Condition for stationarity

anchor.snippet: A necessary and sufficient condition for a time-homogeneous Markov chain to be stationary

annotations.note: The stationarity condition `μ.bind κ = μ` is `Kernel.Invariant`, but no theorem identifying initial-distribution stationarity for time-homogeneous chains is proved.

anchors.snippet: A necessary and sufficient condition for a time-homogeneous Markov chain to be stationary

annotations.label: Markov chain with memory (order m)

anchor.snippet: A Markov chain with memory (or a Markov chain of order m ) where m is finite

annotations.note: No higher-order Markov chain notion is defined in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: A Markov chain with memory (or a Markov chain of order m ) where m is finite

annotations.label: Transition matrix (finite state space)

anchor.snippet: the transition probability distribution can be represented by a matrix , called the transition matrix

annotations.note: Row-stochastic matrices (i.e. transition matrices on a finite state space) are defined as `Matrix.rowStochastic`, but Mathlib lacks an explicit `transitionMatrix` of a Markov chain.

annotations.label: Transition matrix is right stochastic

anchor.snippet: Since each row of P sums to one and all elements are non-negative, P is a right stochastic matrix

annotations.note: `Matrix.mem_rowStochastic_iff_sum` states a matrix is row stochastic iff it has nonneg entries and each row sums to 1.

annotations.label: Stationary distribution

anchor.section: Stationary distribution relation to eigenvectors and simplices
anchor.snippet: A stationary distribution π is a (row) vector, whose entries are non-negative and sum to 1

annotations.note: Invariant measure of a kernel (`Kernel.Invariant κ μ ↔ μ.bind κ = μ`) captures stationary distributions in the kernel setting, but there is no matrix-vector left-eigenvector version.

annotations.label: Stationary distribution as left eigenvector

anchor.section: Stationary distribution relation to eigenvectors and simplices
anchor.snippet: is a normalized

annotations.note: No statement equating a stationary distribution with a normalized left eigenvector of a stochastic matrix is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: k-step transition probability

anchor.section: Time-homogeneous Markov chain with a finite state space
anchor.snippet: the k -step transition probability can be computed as the k -th power of the transition matrix

annotations.note: Matrix powers `A^k` are standard in Mathlib (and used in the irreducibility file to characterize `k`-step reachability), but no Markov-chain-specific `k`-step transition law is stated.

annotations.label: Unique stationary distribution (Perron–Frobenius)

anchor.section: Time-homogeneous Markov chain with a finite state space
anchor.snippet: If the Markov chain is irreducible and aperiodic, then there is a unique stationary distribution

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain a Perron-Frobenius / unique stationary distribution theorem for finite irreducible aperiodic stochastic matrices.

annotations.label: Diagonal-1 row/column persistence

anchor.section: Time-homogeneous Markov chain with a finite state space
anchor.snippet: One thing to notice is that if P has an element

annotations.note: No such persistence-of-diagonal-one lemma for stochastic matrices is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Convergence speed to stationary distribution

anchor.section: Convergence speed to the stationary distribution
anchor.snippet: the stationary (or steady state) distribution π is a left eigenvector of row stochastic matrix P

annotations.note: No convergence-rate / spectral-gap theorem for Markov chains exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continuous-time Markov chain

anchor.snippet: A continuous-time Markov chain

annotations.note: CTMCs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Infinitesimal definition of CTMC

anchor.snippet: be the random variable describing the state of the process at time t

annotations.note: No Q-matrix / infinitesimal generator definition for CTMCs is in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: be the random variable describing the state of the process at time t

annotations.label: Jump chain / holding time definition

anchor.snippet: Define a discrete-time Markov chain Y n to describe the n th jump of the process

annotations.note: No jump-chain or exponential-holding-time construction for CTMCs is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Transition probability definition

anchor.snippet: For any value n = 0, 1, 2, 3, ... and times indexed up to this value of n

annotations.note: The finite-dimensional CTMC transition-probability characterization is not formalized.

annotations.label: Locally interacting Markov chains

anchor.snippet: "Locally interacting Markov chains" are Markov chains with an evolution that takes into account the state of other Markov chains

annotations.note: Locally interacting / interacting particle systems are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Harris chains generalization

anchor.snippet: Many results for discrete-time Markov chains with finite state space can be generalized to chains with uncountable state space through Harris chains

annotations.note: Harris chains and Harris recurrence are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continuous-time Markov process (general state space)

anchor.section: Continuous-time Markov process with general state space
anchor.snippet: A continuous-time Markov process

annotations.note: Continuous-time Markov processes on general state spaces are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Time-homogeneous (weak Markov property)

anchor.section: Continuous-time Markov process with general state space
anchor.snippet: is called time-homogeneous , if it satisfies the weak Markov property for all

annotations.note: No weak Markov property / time-homogeneity predicate for processes is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Transition function / semigroup

anchor.section: Continuous-time Markov process with general state space
anchor.snippet: is the so-called transition function of

annotations.note: Markov transition semigroups for continuous-time processes are not formalized.

annotations.label: Communicating states / irreducibility

anchor.snippet: Two states are said to communicate with each other if both are reachable from one another by a sequence of transitions

annotations.note: Kernel irreducibility is defined as `IsIrreducible φ κ`, and matrix irreducibility via `Matrix.IsIrreducible`; the article's two-state "communicate" relation is not separately defined.

annotations.label: Period of a state

anchor.snippet: A state i has period k if k is the greatest common divisor of the number of transitions

annotations.note: Period of a state is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Periodic / aperiodic state

anchor.snippet: The state is periodic if

annotations.note: Periodicity/aperiodicity of states is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Transient / recurrent state

anchor.snippet: A state i is said to be transient if, starting from i , there is a non-zero probability that the chain will never return to i

annotations.note: Transience and recurrence of states are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mean hitting time

anchor.snippet: For a recurrent state i , the mean hitting time is defined as

annotations.note: Hitting times of stochastic processes are defined (`hittingBtwn`, `hittingAfter`), but the Markov-chain mean return time is not specifically formalized.

annotations.label: Positive / null recurrent

anchor.snippet: State i is positive recurrent if

annotations.note: Positive and null recurrence are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Class properties

anchor.snippet: Periodicity, transience, recurrence and positive and null recurrence are class properties

annotations.note: Class properties of Markov chain states are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Absorbing state

anchor.snippet: A state i is called absorbing if there are no outgoing transitions from the state

annotations.note: Absorbing states are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Irreducibility implies common period

anchor.snippet: Since periodicity is a class property, if a Markov chain is irreducible, then all its states have the same period

annotations.note: No theorem relating irreducibility and a common period is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite irreducible has unique stationary distribution

anchor.snippet: If a finite Markov chain is irreducible, then all states are positive recurrent

annotations.note: The finite-irreducible ⇒ positive recurrent / unique stationary distribution theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ergodic state

anchor.snippet: A state i is said to be ergodic if it is aperiodic and positive recurrent

annotations.note: Per-state ergodicity in the Markov-chain sense is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ergodic Markov chain

anchor.snippet: If all states in an irreducible Markov chain are ergodic, then the chain is said to be ergodic

annotations.note: Mathlib has the general dynamical-system notion `Ergodic f μ`, but not a tailored "ergodic Markov chain" definition.

annotations.label: Finite irreducible chain ergodicity

anchor.snippet: It can be shown that a finite state irreducible Markov chain is ergodic if it has an aperiodic state

annotations.note: No theorem deriving ergodicity from finiteness, irreducibility and aperiodicity is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Single out-going transition obstruction

anchor.snippet: A Markov chain with more than one state and just one out-going transition per state is either not irreducible or not aperiodic

annotations.note: This observation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Primitive / regular matrix

anchor.snippet: Some authors call a matrix primitive if there exists some integer

annotations.note: `Matrix.IsPrimitive A` requires `A` nonnegative and some power `A^k` having all entries strictly positive.

annotations.label: Index of primitivity

anchor.snippet: The index of primitivity , or exponent , of a regular matrix, is the smallest

annotations.note: The smallest `k` such that `A^k > 0` (index of primitivity) is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exponent upper bound

anchor.snippet: The exponent is

annotations.note: The Wielandt-style bound on the exponent of a primitive matrix is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exponent with diagonal entries

anchor.snippet: diagonal entries, then its exponent is

annotations.note: No primitivity-exponent refinement using diagonal entries is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Symmetric matrix exponent

anchor.snippet: is symmetric, then

annotations.note: No exponent bound for symmetric primitive matrices is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dulmage–Mendelsohn theorem

anchor.snippet: (Dulmage-Mendelsohn theorem) The exponent is

annotations.note: The Dulmage-Mendelsohn theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Markov chain as measure-preserving dynamical system

anchor.snippet: If a Markov chain has a stationary distribution, then it can be converted to a measure-preserving dynamical system

annotations.note: Measure-preserving dynamical systems are defined (`MeasurePreserving`), but the specific construction from a stationary Markov chain (shift on path space) is not.

annotations.label: Ergodic dynamical system

anchor.snippet: a measure-preserving dynamical system is called ergodic if any measurable subset

annotations.note: `Ergodic f μ` bundles `MeasurePreserving` with `PreErgodic`, exactly the article's definition.

annotations.label: Irreducibility equivalent to ergodicity of dynamical system

anchor.snippet: the Markov chain is irreducible if its corresponding measure-preserving dynamical system is ergodic

annotations.note: The equivalence between Markov-chain irreducibility and ergodicity of the shift system is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Markovian representation

anchor.snippet: If Y has the Markov property, then it is a Markovian representation of X

annotations.note: Markovian representations of non-Markov processes are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Autoregressive time series Markov representation

anchor.snippet: An example of a non-Markovian process with a Markovian representation is an autoregressive time series of order greater than one

annotations.note: AR(p) processes and their Markov representations are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hitting time

anchor.snippet: The hitting time is the time, starting in a given set of states, until the chain arrives in a given state

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.hittingBtwn` (and `hittingAfter`) define the first time an adapted process enters a set.

annotations.label: Expected hitting times

anchor.snippet: the vector k A of hitting times

annotations.note: The vector of expected hitting times and the linear system characterizing it is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Time reversal is Markov

anchor.snippet: the reverse process

annotations.note: Time-reversed Markov chains are not constructed in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kelly's lemma

anchor.snippet: is a CTMC, then by Kelly's lemma

annotations.note: Kelly's lemma for CTMC reversibility is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reversible chain

anchor.snippet: A chain is said to be reversible if the reversed process is the same as the forward process

annotations.note: `Kernel.IsReversible κ π` is detailed-balance: `∫_A κ x B dπ = ∫_B κ x A dπ`, equivalent to reversibility.

annotations.label: Kolmogorov's criterion

anchor.snippet: Kolmogorov's criterion states that the necessary and sufficient condition for a Markov chain to be reversible

annotations.note: Kolmogorov's reversibility (cycle) criterion is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Embedded Markov chain

anchor.snippet: One method of finding the stationary probability distribution

annotations.note: The embedded jump chain of a CTMC is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Delta-skeleton

anchor.snippet: Another discrete-time process that may be derived from a continuous-time Markov chain is a δ-skeleton

annotations.note: The δ-skeleton sampling of a CTMC is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bernoulli scheme

anchor.snippet: A Bernoulli scheme is a special case of a Markov chain where the transition probability matrix has identical rows

annotations.note: Bernoulli schemes as dynamical systems are not defined in Mathlib (Mathlib has Bernoulli random variables but not the Bernoulli shift system).

annotations.label: Ornstein isomorphism theorem

anchor.snippet: by the Ornstein isomorphism theorem , that every aperiodic and irreducible Markov chain is isomorphic to a Bernoulli scheme

annotations.note: Ornstein's isomorphism theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Topological Markov chain / subshift of finite type

anchor.snippet: When the Markov matrix is replaced by the adjacency matrix of a finite graph , the resulting shift is termed a topological Markov chain

annotations.note: Subshifts and forbidden-pattern subshifts are defined (`Subshift`, `MulSubshift.ofForbidden`), but the specific adjacency-matrix-defined topological Markov chain construction is not formalized.

annotations.label: M/M/1 queue

anchor.snippet: an M/M/1 queue is a CTMC on the non-negative integers

annotations.note: M/M/1 queue is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: PageRank Markov chain

anchor.snippet: The PageRank of a webpage as used by Google is defined by a Markov chain

annotations.note: PageRank is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Drunkard's walk

anchor.snippet: A famous Markov chain is the so-called "drunkard's walk"

annotations.note: The drunkard's walk / simple random walk on integers is not defined as a named Markov chain in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Independent states as Markov chain

anchor.snippet: A series of independent states (for example, a series of coin flips) satisfies the formal definition of a Markov chain

annotations.note: No statement that an i.i.d. sequence is a (degenerate) Markov chain is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mark V. Shaney

anchor.snippet: Mark V. Shaney is a third-order Markov chain program, and a Markov text generator

annotations.note: This computing example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Random walks and gambler's ruin

anchor.snippet: Random walks based on integers and the gambler's ruin problem are examples of Markov processes

annotations.note: Neither generic random walks on the integers nor the gambler's ruin problem are formalized in Mathlib.
